channel.title: Just In
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026, Australian Broadcasting Corporation. All Rights Reserved.

item.title: Why consumers are forking out more for whiting, calamari and garfish
item.description: South Australia's peak seafood body says a short supply of some species due to the algal bloom and commercial fishing restrictions has led to slight price increases, as it warns it could take industry five to 10 years to fully recover.
item.dc:creator: Rachael Merritt

media:group.media:description: King George Whiting, calamari and garfish are slightly more expensive in Adelaide markets.

item.title: Sweeping cuts but little detail — can the government stick to the Abetz budget?
item.description: Since Eric Abetz became Tasmania's treasurer nine months ago, there have been two prevailing questions. How much will be cut? And what will be cut? As of yesterday, we know how much, but don't know what or where.
item.dc:creator: Josh Duggan

media:group.media:description: Tasmanian Treasurer Eric Abetz delivers the 2026-27 state budget.

item.title: 'Stuck between two psychopaths': Cubans describe life as country goes dark
item.description: As power and petrol supplies run out, Cubans tell the ABC about their daily lives. "You can no longer find streets packed with travellers, music and dance," one says. "Only Cuban nationals, still attempting to take some bread home."
item.dc:creator: Emilie Gramenz

media:group.media:description: The streets of Havana are strewn with rubbish, which is no longer being collected.

item.title: Caravan repairers battling to stay afloat as travellers stay close to home
item.description: Normally at this time of year, RVs are hitting the road to head north and escape winter. But the ongoing fuel crisis is forcing travellers to stay closer to home, causing a serious economic downturn for many of the 2,500 businesses in the RV repair industry.
item.dc:creator: Jenae Madden

media:group.media:description: Many thousands of people are employed in the caravan and camping industry nationwide.

item.title: Drivers allege Bruce Highway speed camera used for 'revenue raising'
item.description: The trailer-mounted speed camera has become a regular feature on the Bruce Highway, but these drivers claim they've been "unfairly" targeted in a blatant cash grab.
item.dc:creator: Johanna Marie

media:group.media:description: Some of the drivers who contested their infringements in court.

item.title: AI-generated resumes making hiring 'incredibly difficult', recruiters say
item.description: AI-perfected CVs are becoming the norm as more job applicants and employers use artificial intelligence, but recruiters say the technology could be making hiring trickier.
item.dc:creator: Emma Johnson

media:group.media:description: Research from recruitment agency Michael Page found 67 per cent of job seekers used AI in CVs.

item.title: 'We need more plastic': Australian recyclers call to stop waste exports
item.description: The soft plastic recycling industry wants more waste to stay here, saying too much material is being sent overseas.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Paul

media:group.media:description: Recycling company director Clive Townsend says more waste should stay in Australia for local processing.

item.title: Early lung damage in kids with CF linked with immune cell changes
item.description: Australian researchers have mapped how lung damage may begin early in life for children with cystic fibrosis, providing new insights that may help reshape future care.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Roberts

media:group.media:description: Lewis took part in the study when he was a preschooler.

item.title: Snowy bosses paid $1.2m in bonuses as hydro project costs spiral
item.description: The renewables project's chief executive, Dennis Barnes, received $323,000 in bonus pay on top of his $1.69 million salary last financial year, while four other senior executives received about $919,000 in "variable pay".
item.dc:creator: Jake Evans

media:group.media:description: Snowy Hydro chief executive Dennis Barnes is one of the highest-paid public servants in Australia.

item.title: One Nation surfs wave as Labor defends budget
item.description: Before the budget, Anthony Albanese and Jim Chalmers knew they would be hit by criticism for its broken promises, but they underestimated the extent and ferocity of the backlash.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Grattan

media:group.media:description: One Nation has its tail up with post budget polls showing rises in its support.

item.title: Treasurer asked to explain productivity impact of tax changes
item.description: The Productivity Commission was not asked to model how changes to capital gains tax would impact Australia's economy. Business groups say Treasurer Jim Chalmers must explain how the government concluded its tax changes would ultimately be a boon to productivity.
item.dc:creator: Jake Evans

media:group.media:description: Treasurer Jim Chalmers says the federal budget will boost productivity over time.

item.title: What we know about Iran's new system of transit in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Iran is asserting unprecedented control over the strategically critical waterway, with ships reportedly facing screening processes, transit approvals and potential Bitcoin-backed insurance requirements.
item.dc:creator: Audrey Courty

media:group.media:description: Iran claims it is already coordinating vessel movements through the Srait of Hormuz.

item.title: 'It's not a secret': Inside the town where people don't exist
item.description: In a community like no other, what happens when people aren't counted?
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Story Carter and Catherine McAloon

media:group.media:description: A worker sits in a makeshift home in Robinvale.

item.title: How women became central to prosecutions over IS slavery
item.description: Australia is the latest country to prosecute its own citizens over Islamic State's system of Yazidi slavery, part of a growing global reckoning over the role some foreign women allegedly played in the atrocities.
item.dc:creator: Audrey Courty

media:group.media:description: Kawsar Abbas (left) and Zeinab Ahmad are facing slavery-related charges.

item.title: ASIO cut counterterrorism funding share to post-9/11 low before Bondi
item.description: Australia's intelligence agencies had cut counterterrorism funding to its lowest share this century before the Bondi massacre, despite warnings that a terrorist attack was probable and that antisemitic violence posed an escalating threat to life.
item.dc:creator: Sean Rubinsztein-Dunlop

media:group.media:description: ASIO director-general Mike Burgess is expected to appear at the next block of royal commission hearings.

item.title: Friday news quiz: A pumped prince, a grinning sculpture and a stowaway frog
item.description: You'll be as happy as Prince William when you take this week's quiz, which goes off like a frog in a … bag of lettuce.

media:group.media:description: Prince William goes wild in the stands as Aston Villa score against Freiburg in the Europa League final.

item.title: Drone shows return to Vivid Sydney after crowd-crush fears
item.description: Dazzling drone shows will return to Sydney's largest art and culture festival for the first time since 2024.
item.dc:creator: Phoebe Pin

media:group.media:description: Drones have not featured at Vivid since 2024.

item.title: The hidden codes and Aussie influence in Harry Connick Jr's new symphony
item.description: Harry Connick Jr's new symphony, Elaboratio, is filled with musical tributes to his mother Anita. Orchestra Victoria and Australian conductor Jessica Gethin are bringing those memories to life at Carnegie Hall.
item.dc:creator: Megan Burslem

media:group.media:description: Hollywood star and veteran jazz musician Harry Connick Jr has written his first symphony.

item.title: Air France and Airbus fined for 2009 crash that killed 228
item.description: France's national airline Air France and Airbus have both been found guilty of involuntary manslaughter over a 2009 crash that killed 228 people.

media:group.media:description: Air France and Airbus have both been found guilty of involuntary manslaughter over a 2009 crash.

item.title: Another group of Australians with IS links leave camp in Syria for Australia
item.description: The ABC has seen buses carrying the group, which left the Al Roj camp on Thursday afternoon local time.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Doran

media:group.media:description: A bus carrying Australian women and children left the Al Roj camp on Thursday afternoon, local time.

item.title: The Mushroom Tapes, Mad Mabel and Memorial Days triumph at book awards
item.description: In May 2025, Helen Garner, Chloe Hooper and Sarah Krasnostein, three literary true-crime writers, headed to Erin Patterson's trial. The resulting book just won an Australian Book Industry Award.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Story

media:group.media:description: Three respected authors — Sarah Krasnostein, Helen Garner and Chloe Hooper — joined forces to write the now-award-winning The Mushroom Tapes.

item.title: Pauline Hanson caught swearing as staffer says 'shut up' to journalist
item.description: Pauline Hanson and her party have again been caught in tense moments with media, with the One Nation leader captured on camera swearing and a staffer telling a journalist to "shut up".

media:group.media:description: One Nation Leader Pauline Hanson was in Adelaide for an oil and gas conference.

item.title: Police arrest man after alleged threats to carry out Bourke St attack
item.description: A man who allegedly threatened to carry out an incident in Melbourne's Bourke Street tonight has been arrested by police.

media:group.media:description: Victoria Police on patrol in Melbourne.

item.title: Nampijinpa Price: 'Extremist ideology' destroying Australian culture
item.description: Jacinta Nampijinpa Price has denied that she agreed with a podcaster saying Australia should stop 'flooding' Australia with migrants from Asia, Africa and the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Clare Armstrong and Sara Tomevska

media:group.media:description: Jacinta Nampijinpa Price says if people want to come to Australia they have to "adopt our values".

item.title: Queen was 'very keen' her son Andrew got UK trade job, documents reveal
item.description: The historic documents show no security vetting took place at the time.

media:group.media:description: Queen Elizabeth II expressed a 'wish' that her son Andrew 'take on a prominent role'.
